title: Setting up and using a shaving horse
subtitle: How to position, clamp, and work at the shaving horse
excerpt: A guide to setting up the shaving horse correctly for drawknife and spokeshave work. Covers seat height, foot-pedal position, workpiece orientation, and the clamping feedback loop. The foundation for any green-woodwork session at the horse.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for shaving-horse setup and the bodger's working position. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on the English bodger tradition. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for the bench-height and clamping principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A foot-pedalled bench that clamps a workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece.

content.text: The
shaving horse
does one thing: it clamps the workpiece so both your hands are free for the
drawknife

or spokeshave. Getting the position right before starting means less fatigue, better clamping force, and cleaner cuts. An incorrectly set horse means the work slips, the cuts go wrong, and the session is frustrating.
Seat height

The bench surface (where the workpiece rests) should be at about mid-thigh height when you are seated on the bench. Your feet should rest flat on the foot pedal with your knees at roughly 90 degrees. A horse that is too high means your arms are working downward at an awkward angle; too low means you are hunching forward.
Most horses have an adjustable seat notch. If yours does not, build up the bench height with a piece of planed oak under the rear legs, or sit on a cushion to raise your position.
The clamping action

Place the workpiece on the bench surface with the section you are working on projecting forward past the dumb-head clamp. Press the foot pedal forward until the dumb-head drops onto the workpiece and clamps it firmly. The clamping force increases the harder you press the pedal.

The key principle: the drawknife pull direction should be roughly parallel to the bench surface. If you are pulling slightly downward as the blade travels, the clamping force increases as the workpiece tries to slip toward you. This is the feedback loop that makes the shaving horse work; it gets tighter the harder you pull.
Workpiece orientation
For drawknife work, the workpiece grain should run along the bench length. Cuts travel with the grain from the far end toward your body. Rotate the workpiece after each pass to work the next face.

For spokeshave work, the workpiece can be oriented at an angle if the grain runs at a diagonal. Spokeshaves follow shorter strokes; orient the work so each stroke ends with the grain rather than against it.
Repositioning the workpiece

Release the foot pedal completely to reposition the workpiece. Do not try to shift the workpiece while maintaining partial foot pressure; the clamp will not release cleanly and the dumb-head may mark the wood.
Working stance and fatigue

A drawknife session of more than two hours is genuinely tiring on the forearms and shoulders. Take a ten-minute break every forty-five minutes. Stretch the forearms and shoulders before returning. Cramped forearms lead to inconsistent pressure and poorly controlled cuts.
Troubleshooting

If the workpiece slips during a pull stroke, the drawknife is pulling upward as well as toward you. Lower your grip angle so the blade travels parallel to the bench surface. If the clamp marks the wood, pad the dumb-head face with a thin piece of leather.
